title: Unrestricted File Upload
summary: An avatar upload stores files and serves them back. Five stages validate a little better each time — no checks, a case-sensitive extension blacklist, a client-MIME check, echoing the client Content-Type, and a double-extension mix-up — until the server decides the name, type, and adds nosniff.
goal: Upload a file that gets served back with an executable Content-Type (text/html or SVG) so a browser runs your script — stored XSS via upload.
goalSecure: Try the earlier tricks (.html, .HTML, spoofed MIME, client type, avatar.html.png) — all should be served inert, while a real image still uploads.
rootCause: The upload's name and served Content-Type are influenced by attacker-controlled input, so a file can be served as executable content instead of an inert image.
lessons: Serving user files with an executable Content-Type turns an upload into stored XSS.
A case-sensitive extension blacklist is bypassed by changing case.
The client-supplied Content-Type is attacker-controlled and proves nothing.
Even a safe extension is unsafe if the file is served with the client's Content-Type.
Multi-dotted names split the validator and the server (avatar.html.png).
The fix: server-generated name, allowlisted final extension, server-chosen type, and X-Content-Type-Options: nosniff.

stages.title: No validation
stages.defense: None — any file is stored and served by extension.

stages.title: Trusts the client Content-Type
stages.defense: Requires the claimed Content-Type to be image/*.

stages.title: Allowlisted extension, client type on serve
stages.defense: Requires an image extension; serves with the claimed Content-Type.

stages.title: Double extension
stages.defense: Allowlists the final extension; serves by the first one.

stages.title: Server-decided name, type, and nosniff
stages.defense: Allowlist the final extension; random name; server-chosen image type; X-Content-Type-Options: nosniff.
